disease.name: Pediatric Tuberculosis
disease.note: In children, hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy is more common than parenchymal disease. Primary TB is the dominant form. Young children (<2 years) are at highest risk of disseminated (miliary) and meningeal TB.

immediateDangerSigns.description: Neck stiffness, bulging fontanelle, altered consciousness, seizures

severeCriteria.action: Admit, treat with prolonged regimen (9-12 months), add corticosteroids for TB meningitis

nonSevereCriteria.description: Chronic cough >2 weeks, weight loss/failure to thrive, typical CXR findings (hilar LAD, primary complex), no danger signs

historyFeatures.note: Persistent cough >14 days is a WHO screening criterion
Weight loss is often more prominent than cough in children
Household contact with smear-positive TB is the single strongest risk factor
Especially if poor response to standard antibiotics
BCG provides partial protection against severe TB (miliary, meningitis) in children
Matted non-tender nodes, often in cervical chain

examFeatures.note: May be absent or minimal in pediatric TB; often overshadowed by lymphadenopathy
Cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary. Matted, non-tender, firm.
Weight <3rd centile or crossing centiles downward
Dullness, reduced breath sounds, mediastinal shift
Only if advanced or significant airway compression from nodes
Neck stiffness, Kernig/Brudzinski signs, altered consciousness — suspect TBM

riskFactors.note: HIV increases TB risk and complicates treatment
Severe acute malnutrition is a major risk factor
Strongest risk factor for pediatric TB
Young children are more susceptible to progression from latent to active TB

keyFeatures.description: Most common radiologic finding in children

subtypes.name: Miliary (Disseminated) TB

keyFeatures.description: Diffuse 1-2 mm nodules throughout both lung fields

subtypes.management: Prolonged regimen (9-12 months). Corticosteroids if CNS or pericardial involvement.

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Meningitis (TBM)
subtypes.management: Prolonged anti-TB therapy (9-12 months) + corticosteroids (prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day, taper over 4-6 weeks). ICU care.

subtypes.name: TB Lymphadenitis (Scrofula)
subtypes.management: Standard 2RHZE/4RH regimen. Surgical excision only for diagnosis or large cold abscess.

subtypes.name: Tuberculous Pleural Effusion
subtypes.management: Standard 2RHZE/4RH regimen. Therapeutic thoracentesis if respiratory compromise.

mimics.note: TB and PCP can coexist in HIV-infected children

bedside.name: WHO symptom screening (4-question)
bedside.indication: cough >2 weeks, fever, weight loss, night sweats

laboratory.name: GeneXpert MTB/RIF on sputum or gastric aspirate
laboratory.note: Gastric aspirate preferred in young children who cannot expectorate. Collect 3 early morning specimens. Detects rifampicin resistance.

laboratory.name: AFB smear microscopy x 3 (sputum or gastric aspirate)
TST (Mantoux tuberculin skin test)
laboratory.interpretation: ≥10 mm = positive in non-HIV, ≥5 mm = positive in HIV-infected or severely malnourished children
laboratory.note: False negative possible in severe malnutrition, HIV, miliary TB

laboratory.name: IGRA (Interferon-Gamma Release Assay)
laboratory.note: More specific than TST in BCG-vaccinated children; cannot distinguish latent vs active TB

laboratory.name: HIV test (PCR for <18 months, rapid for older)
LFT baseline (ALT, AST, bilirubin)
laboratory.note: Essential due to hepatotoxicity of INH, RIF, PZA

laboratory.name: CSF analysis (if TBM suspected)
laboratory.findings: Lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose. GeneXpert on CSF.

typicalFindings.primary_tb: hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy (more common than parenchymal changes in children), Ghon complex, segmental hyperinflation or atelectasis
typicalFindings.miliary: diffuse 1-2 mm micronodules throughout both lung fields
typicalFindings.older_child_cavitation: apical cavitation (more common in children >5 years)

imaging.name: CT chest
imaging.indication: diagnostic doubt, evaluate lymphadenopathy, complications (bronchiectasis, airway compression)

advanced.name: Gastric aspirate for GeneXpert and culture
advanced.note: Collect 3 early morning specimens before feeds

management.protocolReference: Kenya National Guidelines for Management of Tuberculosis in Children + WHO Pediatric TB Guidelines

pathways.criteria: GeneXpert MTB detected, RIF sensitive. No severe/disseminated disease.
treatment: Intensive phase: 2 months of RHZE (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) daily
Continuation phase: 4 months of RH (Rifampicin, Isoniazid) daily

pathways.criteria: TB meningitis, miliary TB, spinal TB, osteoarticular TB
treatment: Continuation phase: 7-10 months RH daily (total 9-12 months)
Adjunct corticosteroids: Prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day PO for TB meningitis, taper over 4-6 weeks
All-oral MDR-TB regimen per WHO guidelines (6-9 months): Bedaquiline, Linezolid, Levofloxacin, Clofazimine, Cycloserine
Individualized treatment based on DST pattern

weightBasedDosing.note: Dosing per WHO weight bands for pediatric TB. Critical to calculate correctly — overdose risks toxicity, underdose risks treatment failure and resistance.

indications: Child <5 years with household TB contact, asymptomatic with negative TB workup
tbPreventiveTherapy.regimen: 6 months of Isoniazid 10 mg/kg/day (6H) OR 3 months of weekly Rifapentine + INH (3HP) for children ≥2 years

supportiveCare: Nutritional assessment and supplementation — weight gain is the best marker of treatment response
HIV care if co-infected: cotrimoxazole prophylaxis, ART initiation (usually 2-8 weeks after starting TB treatment)
Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) 12.5-25 mg/day to prevent INH-induced peripheral neuropathy
Monitor LFTs monthly — INH, RIF, PZA all hepatotoxic
management.followUp: Monthly weight, symptom review, and adherence check. Repeat CXR at 2 months (end of intensive phase) and at treatment completion.

treatmentResponseLogic.expectedImprovement: Clinical improvement (weight gain, reduced cough, reduced fever) within 2-4 weeks. Weight gain is often the best early marker of response in children.

actions: Repeat GeneXpert and culture for drug susceptibility testing
Re-evaluate for alternative diagnoses: HIV-related lung disease, malignancy, bronchiectasis, aspiration
Consider malabsorption of TB drugs (especially in malnourished children)
If confirmed RR/MDR-TB, switch to second-line regimen

complications.note: Enlarged hilar lymph nodes can compress airways in young children
Long-term complication of inadequately treated TB
Monitor LFTs regularly during TB treatment

disease.emergencyPriority: TB meningitis, miliary TB, and severe respiratory distress require immediate admission and multidisciplinary care
